raw_sources.name: Aaron Ross
raw_sources.perspective: Revenue becomes predictable only when prospecting and closing are separated into specialized roles, leads are categorized by source type with distinct economics, and pipeline output is directly proportional to measured inputs.
raw_sources.credentials: Built the outbound sales team at Salesforce.com that added $100M+ in recurring revenue, author of 'Predictable Revenue' (the 'Sales Bible of Silicon Valley') and 'From Impossible to Inevitable,' founder of Predictable Revenue Inc.

raw_sources.name: Adrienne Barnes
raw_sources.perspective: If 85% of companies create buyer personas but 77% never use them, the problem is not that personas do not work; the problem is that traditional personas are built on demographics instead of Jobs to Be Done, producing documents that inform nothing and inspire no action.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Best Buyer Persona; B2B SaaS content marketing consultant who has worked with Stripe, Monday.com, Unbounce, Demio, Audiense, and Smartling; self-taught qualitative researcher who studied interview methodology from social workers, journalists, and product researchers; expert in Jobs to Be Done-based persona development.

raw_sources.name: Alex Hormozi
raw_sources.perspective: The offer is the single highest-leverage variable in business; a Grand Slam Offer so good people feel stupid saying no makes every other growth lever -- traffic, conversion, sales skill -- dramatically more effective.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder of Acquisition.com, a portfolio of companies generating $200M+ in annual revenue. Author of '$100M Offers' and '$100M Leads'. Built and scaled Gym Launch from zero to $100M+ in cumulative revenue. Investor in and advisor to dozens of portfolio companies across service, SaaS, and education verticals.

raw_sources.name: Andrew Faris
raw_sources.perspective: Every brand advertising on Meta should spend the vast majority of their dollars on cost controls; the biggest ad cost in most DTC businesses is creative testing, and cost caps solve this by letting Meta suppress spend on likely losers automatically.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of AJF Growth (ecommerce growth consultancy); former host of the Ecommerce Playbook podcast at Common Thread Collective; has grown brands including The North Face, Melin, TheraGun, DIFF Eyewear, Born Primitive, and Axe Bat; specializes in Meta ads for ecommerce brands in the $1M-$20M growth stage.

raw_sources.name: Andy Crestodina
raw_sources.perspective: The best content programs differentiate through original research and strong opinions, not volume; AI cannot replicate either, so the future belongs to marketers who take a stand and create data that does not exist elsewhere.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder and CMO of Orbit Media Studios; author of Content Chemistry (7th edition); has published 500+ articles on content strategy, SEO, and analytics; runs the annual Blogger Survey (10+ years, 1000+ respondents); LinkedIn newsletter with 215,000+ subscribers.

raw_sources.name: Ann Handley
raw_sources.perspective: Good writing is not a nice-to-have but the fundamental skill of modern marketing; every piece of content is an extension of your brand, and the quality bar is Utility times Inspiration times Empathy, where any factor at zero produces nothing.
raw_sources.credentials: Chief Content Officer of MarketingProfs; Wall Street Journal bestselling author of Everybody Writes (2nd edition, 2022) and Content Rules; creator of Total Annarchy newsletter (42,000+ subscribers); named Forbes Top 20 Marketing and Social Media Women Bloggers; pioneer of the Chief Content Officer title.

raw_sources.name: Arvid Kahl
raw_sources.perspective: Build calm and sustainable businesses in public by embedding yourself in a community first, finding their critical problems, and using AI as a 10-80-10 workflow accelerant -- you do the first 10% (direction), AI does the middle 80% (execution), and you do the final 10% (quality and polish).
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founded FeedbackPanda (bootstrapped SaaS to $55K MRR, sold within 2 years), author of 'Zero to Sold' and 'The Embedded Entrepreneur,' creator of The Bootstrapped Founder newsletter and podcast, builder of Podscan

raw_sources.name: Ben Tossell
raw_sources.perspective: AI is the co-pilot, not the autopilot -- the path to a profitable AI business is teaching others to use tools effectively, building in public, and structuring your business around what you love doing while automating what you hate.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Ben's Bites (7-figure AI education business, 100K+ subscribers), previously founded Makerpad (no-code education platform, acquired by Zapier), Product Hunt community manager, now Head of DevRel at Factory AI

raw_sources.name: Bernadette Jiwa
raw_sources.perspective: You don't need to compete when you know who you are. Innovation is a by-product of empathy. People don't buy products; they buy the story behind them. The difference between a brand and a commodity is meaning: Product + Meaning = Brand; Product - Meaning = Commodity.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of ten #1 Amazon bestsellers on marketing and brand storytelling, including 'Difference,' 'Story Driven,' 'Meaningful,' 'The Fortune Cookie Principle,' 'Marketing: A Love Story,' and 'Make Your Idea Matter.' Her blog TheStoryofTelling.com was voted Best Australian Business Blog (2012) and named one of Australia's Top 20 Business Blogs (2013, 2014). Consults with global brands including Disney, Adidas, Microsoft, Google, and Lululemon. Called 'the master of making organizational change around marketing' by Seth Godin.

raw_sources.name: Bernard Huang
raw_sources.perspective: Content quality for SEO is measurable through NLP analysis of semantic comprehensiveness; by analyzing what the top-ranking pages cover and how they cover it, you can provide data-driven guidance to writers that dramatically improves content relevance without requiring them to become SEO experts.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder and CEO of Clearscope (founded 2016), built the content optimization category that now includes competitors like Surfer SEO and MarketMuse, Clearscope is used by Adobe, Shopify, YouTube, Intuit, and Conde Nast, pioneered the application of NLP (Google NLP API and IBM Watson) to SEO content grading

raw_sources.name: Blair Enns
raw_sources.perspective: The highest financial success comes not from selling time efficiently but from mastering the value conversation, where you price the client and not the service.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'The Win Without Pitching Manifesto', 'Pricing Creativity', and 'The Four Conversations'. Founder of Win Without Pitching. Co-host of 2Bobs podcast with David C. Baker. Trains creative firms worldwide on selling and pricing expertise.

raw_sources.name: Brian Dean
raw_sources.perspective: SEO success comes from creating content that genuinely deserves to rank number one, then systematically promoting it to people who can amplify it; the best link building is a byproduct of being the best resource on a topic.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Backlinko (acquired by Semrush), creator of the Skyscraper Technique, Semrush Academy instructor for Content-Led SEO course, built Backlinko to millions of monthly visitors through content-led SEO without paid advertising

raw_sources.name: Charlie Munger
raw_sources.perspective: The best way to achieve wisdom is to develop a latticework of mental models from multiple disciplines and use them in combination; the person who has only one way of thinking is dangerous to themselves and everyone around them.

Author of Poor Charlie's Almanack. Trained as a lawyer (Harvard Law) and meteorologist (Caltech). Considered one of the greatest investors and multi-disciplinary thinkers of the 20th and 21st centuries.

raw_sources.name: Chris Do
raw_sources.perspective: Amateurs give advice, experts diagnose; the path to premium creative pricing is learning to ask questions that reveal the client's definition of value before you ever propose a solution.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder and CEO of The Futur, an education platform for creative professionals with millions of followers. Emmy award-winning designer. Former creative director of Blind, a motion design studio in Los Angeles. Author and speaker on pricing, business, and design.

raw_sources.name: Chris Voss
raw_sources.perspective: All decision-making is emotionally driven, and the most effective way to influence outcomes is not through logic or compromise but through tactical empathy, calibrated questions, and giving the other party the illusion of control.

raw_sources.name: Chris Walker
raw_sources.perspective: B2B marketing should prioritize demand creation through dark social channels over demand capture through attribution-trackable lead gen; the companies that win are the ones that build brand preference before buyers enter the market.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder and CEO of Passetto (GTM strategy consultancy) and Chairman of Refine Labs; has worked with 100+ B2B SaaS companies; host of the State of Demand Gen podcast; created the HIRO Pipeline metric and Split the Funnel framework.

raw_sources.name: Christoph Janz

raw_sources.credentials: General Partner and Co-founder of Point Nine Capital, one of Europe's most prominent early-stage SaaS-focused VC firms. Writes at christophjanz.blogspot.com (The Angel VC) and saas.wtf.

raw_sources.name: Claire Suellentrop
raw_sources.perspective: The most powerful growth lever in SaaS is understanding the job your best customers hired your product to do, then systematically aligning every touchpoint -- from onboarding email to pricing page -- to that job.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder of Forget the Funnel, co-author of 'Forget the Funnel: A Customer-Led Approach for Driving Predictable, Recurring Revenue' (25,000+ copies sold), former Director of Marketing at Calendly, co-creator of the Customer-Led Growth framework used by 200+ SaaS companies including Wistia, SparkToro, and Appcues

raw_sources.name: Codie Sanchez
raw_sources.perspective: The fastest path to financial freedom is not building a startup from scratch but acquiring an existing 'boring' business with proven cash flow, because buying removes the highest-risk phase of entrepreneurship entirely.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Contrarian Thinking and co-founder of Unconventional Acquisitions. Former Wall Street executive (First Trust, Goldman Sachs, State Street, Vanguard). NYT bestselling author of 'Main Street Millionaire'. Runs Main Street Holding Company, a portfolio of 'boring businesses' including laundromats, car washes, and service businesses. Has taught 10,000+ people how to buy businesses. 3.5M+ newsletter subscribers.

raw_sources.name: Cole Schafer
raw_sources.perspective: Copy should open hearts and wallets. People buy for exactly one reason: to move closer to pleasure or further from pain. Everything else is noise. You cannot bore someone into buying.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Honeycopy, a conversion copywriting writers' room. Poet and copywriter. Known for combining literary voice with commercial effectiveness. Has written for brands including major DTC and B2B companies. Published essayist and newsletter writer with a large following.

raw_sources.name: Crayon
raw_sources.perspective: The CI practitioner's job is not to be the all-knowing competitive expert but to identify who is winning against each competitor and enable the entire team to learn from them — making battlecards a peer-learning system, not a top-down intelligence report.
raw_sources.credentials: Competitive intelligence software platform trusted by Gong, Dropbox, and ZoomInfo; publishes the annual State of Competitive Intelligence report; primary author Conor Bond on the Crayon marketing team.

raw_sources.name: Cyrus Shepard
raw_sources.perspective: The most reliable SEO insights come from large-scale data studies and firsthand experience with Google's quality evaluation systems; understanding how Google actually rates quality, not how SEOs assume it does, is the key competitive advantage.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Zyppy SEO, former Chief SEO Strategist at Moz, worked as a Google Search Quality Rater, conducted landmark studies on title tag rewrites (81,000 tags) and internal linking (23 million links across 1,800 sites), regular speaker at MozCon, SearchLove, and BrightonSEO

raw_sources.name: Dan Ariely
raw_sources.perspective: Humans are predictably irrational; we cannot perceive absolute values, only relative ones, and every pricing decision is shaped by anchoring, decoy effects, the power of 'free,' and the pain of paying, all of which can be systematically exploited or mitigated through deliberate choice architecture.
raw_sources.credentials: James B. Duke Professor of Psychology and Behavioral Economics at Duke University. Bestselling author of 'Predictably Irrational,' 'The Upside of Irrationality,' and 'Dollars and Sense.' Founded the Center for Advanced Hindsight. His research on irrational decision-making has been cited over 100,000 times and directly influenced pricing strategy across industries.

raw_sources.name: Daniel Kahneman
raw_sources.perspective: Human judgment is systematically flawed by predictable cognitive biases; the overconfident System 1 generates intuitions that System 2 is too lazy to override, and the result is that we are reliably wrong in identifiable patterns.
raw_sources.credentials: Nobel laureate in Economics (2002) for work on prospect theory. Professor emeritus of psychology at Princeton University. Author of Thinking, Fast and Slow and co-author of Noise: A Flaw in Human Judgment. Pioneered behavioral economics with Amos Tversky. One of the most cited psychologists in history.

raw_sources.name: Dave Gerhardt
raw_sources.perspective: Brand is how you get people to know, like, and trust you before they buy; in B2B, your story IS your strategy, and the companies that win are the ones that build a movement around a point of view rather than just shipping campaigns.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder and CEO of Exit Five (the largest private community for B2B marketers); former VP Marketing at Drift ($1B exit) and CMO at Privy ($100M+ exit); host of the B2B Marketing with Dave Gerhardt podcast; built DGMG into a media brand for B2B marketing education.

raw_sources.name: Dave Harland
raw_sources.perspective: Standing out requires sounding like nobody else. Write like YOU speak, not how people generally speak. Your quirks are your competitive advantage. People don't want to know how proud you are of your company. They want to know how you'll change their life.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of The Word Man; creative copywriter for Emirates, Jaguar, Nespresso, Red Bull, BBC, and other major brands. Known as one of LinkedIn's most distinctive voices on B2B copywriting. Specialist in brand voice and personality-driven commercial copy.

raw_sources.name: David Aaker
raw_sources.perspective: The only way to achieve real growth is to develop offerings so innovative that they create new categories or subcategories, making competitors irrelevant. Brand relevance, not brand preference, is the path to market leadership. Stop competing on being better; compete on being the only relevant option.
raw_sources.credentials: Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley's Haas School of Business. Vice Chairman of Prophet, a global brand and marketing consultancy. Author of 18 books including 'Managing Brand Equity,' 'Building Strong Brands,' 'Brand Relevance,' 'Aaker on Branding,' and 'Brand Portfolio Strategy.' Often called the 'Father of Modern Branding.' His Brand Equity Model is taught in virtually every MBA marketing program worldwide. Received the Paul D. Converse Award for outstanding contributions to marketing.

raw_sources.name: David C. Baker
raw_sources.perspective: Expertise is the product of narrow positioning; insight flows from focus, and the less exchangeable you are, the higher the premium you command.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'The Business of Expertise'. Advisory and M&A for marketing firms via Punctuation (formerly ReCourses). Worked with 900+ firms over 30 years. Co-host of 2Bobs podcast with Blair Enns.

raw_sources.name: David Skok
raw_sources.perspective: A SaaS business is only viable if the lifetime value of a customer significantly exceeds the cost to acquire them; the 3:1 LTV:CAC ratio and months-to-recover-CAC are the two metrics that determine long-term profitability and growth potential.
raw_sources.credentials: Serial entrepreneur turned VC, General Partner at Matrix Partners. Five-time entrepreneur with four successful exits. His blog forEntrepreneurs.com is the definitive reference for SaaS metrics and unit economics, read by hundreds of thousands of founders and investors. His 'SaaS Metrics 2.0' post is arguably the single most influential piece of SaaS business writing ever published.

raw_sources.name: Debbie Millman
raw_sources.perspective: Branding is simply a fancy word for communication. But it is a deeply human act rooted in 7,000 years of humans marking, organizing, and signaling who they are and where they belong. Brands are tribal identifiers. We don't buy products; we buy into what they represent about our identity, our values, and our place in the world.
raw_sources.credentials: Host and founder of Design Matters, the first and longest-running podcast about design (20+ years, 500+ episodes). Former President of the design division at Sterling Brands, where she redesigned over 200 global brands including Pepsi, Burger King, Colgate, Nestlé, Campbell's, and Hasbro. Author of seven books including 'Brand Thinking and Other Noble Pursuits,' 'How to Think Like a Great Graphic Designer,' and 'Why Design Matters.' Chair of the School of Visual Arts MFA in Branding program. Named one of the most creative people in business by Fast Company and one of the most influential designers working today by Graphic Design USA.

raw_sources.name: Denise Lee Yohn
raw_sources.perspective: Great brands start inside. The most powerful competitive advantage comes not from your external brand identity or your internal culture alone, but from fusing the two into a single, interdependent system. When what you do on the inside matches what you say on the outside, you become unstoppable.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'What Great Brands Do: The Seven Brand-Building Principles that Separate the Best from the Rest' and 'FUSION: How Integrating Brand and Culture Powers the World's Greatest Companies.' Twenty-five years of consulting work with top brands including Frito-Lay, Sony, Nautica, Burger King, Jack in the Box, and Zimmer Biomet. Named one of the world's leading brand experts by multiple industry surveys. Regular contributor to Harvard Business Review, Forbes, and Fast Company.

raw_sources.name: Devin Reed
raw_sources.perspective: Great B2B marketing is not about getting as many people as possible to see your message; it is about getting as many people in your market to see it, which means building for resonance with buyers, not popularity with everyone, and treating content as a revenue function, not a brand awareness exercise.
raw_sources.credentials: 2x Head of Content at Gong (grew from $20M to $200M ARR) and Clari; founder of The Reeder (14,000+ newsletter subscribers); created Clari CEO's LinkedIn strategy generating 6.1M views in 12 months; SaaS advisor; speaker at Content Marketing World.

raw_sources.name: Donald Miller
raw_sources.perspective: People don't buy the best products; they buy the products they can understand the fastest. If you confuse, you lose. The customer is the hero, not your brand. Your brand is the guide.
raw_sources.credentials: CEO of StoryBrand (now part of the Business Made Simple platform). New York Times bestselling author of 'Building a StoryBrand' and 'Marketing Made Simple.' His StoryBrand framework has been used by more than 500,000 businesses. Built the StoryBrand Certified Guide network of marketing professionals.

raw_sources.name: Doug Holt
raw_sources.perspective: Iconic brands are not built through benefits, personalities, or emotional connections. They succeed by forging deep connections with culture, creating identity myths that soothe collective anxieties arising from acute social change.
raw_sources.credentials: Former professor at Harvard Business School and L'Oreal Chair of Marketing at Oxford. Author of 'How Brands Become Icons' and co-author of 'Cultural Strategy.' raw_sources.name: Eddie Shleyner

raw_sources.credentials: Founder of VeryGoodCopy; former Senior Copywriter at G2 where he achieved 60%+ conversion rates on landing pages; has written for Gong, Amplitude, Justin Welsh, and other high-growth SaaS companies. Teaches micro-lessons on copywriting craft to 100K+ subscribers.

raw_sources.name: Elad Gil

raw_sources.credentials: Serial entrepreneur (co-founder of Color Genomics and Mixer Labs), former VP at Twitter, early Google employee. Angel investor in Airbnb, Coinbase, Stripe, Square, Instacart, and others. Author of 'High Growth Handbook' (Stripe Press), one of the most widely-read startup scaling guides. Substack blog with tens of thousands of subscribers.

raw_sources.name: Else van der Berg
raw_sources.perspective: Data shows what is happening, never why — the why lives in qualitative context, and AI tools that merge both are transformative
raw_sources.credentials: Product coach, 50+ teams coached over 4 years, Substack author

raw_sources.name: Emily Kramer
raw_sources.perspective: Positioning is about producing the right answer — a clear comparison — not about showing your work; most companies get lost in the research and never synthesize it into a simple, differentiated statement.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of MKT1; former VP Marketing at Asana and Carta; writes the MKT1 Newsletter, one of the most widely-read B2B marketing newsletters.

raw_sources.name: Gaetano DiNardi
raw_sources.perspective: Great demand generation is lead quality, not lead volume; you dominate your niche by producing the best educational content, owning review site presence, and building a video-first content engine that double-occupies SERP real estate.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Marketing Advice (B2B SaaS growth consultancy); former VP of Growth Marketing at Nextiva and Head of Growth at Salesforce/Cognism; contributor to CXL, G2, and Advanced Web Ranking; specialist in SEO-driven demand generation for B2B SaaS.

raw_sources.name: Georgiana Laudi

raw_sources.name: Greg Isenberg

raw_sources.name: Hermann Simon
raw_sources.perspective: Price is the most powerful profit lever and the most neglected; a 1% price increase drives 8-11% profit improvement, yet most companies lack a formal pricing process, a pricing department, or even a pricing-literate CEO.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder and Honorary Chairman of Simon-Kucher & Partners, the world's largest pricing consultancy (1,800+ professionals, 40+ offices globally). raw_sources.name: Hiten Shah
raw_sources.perspective: Product-market fit is not a destination but a continuous process of customer research; the best SaaS companies obsessively uncover the 'pattern of pain,' validate with qualitative surveys, and let product quality drive retention and growth before worrying about strategy or business model.

raw_sources.name: Jacco van der Kooij

raw_sources.name: James Clear
raw_sources.perspective: You do not rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems. True behavior change is identity change: the goal is not to read a book, the goal is to become a reader.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of Atomic Habits (15M+ copies sold, translated into 50+ languages). Creator of jamesclear.com, one of the most-visited personal development sites. Speaker on habits, decision-making, and continuous improvement. Former athlete and entrepreneur.

raw_sources.name: Jason Fried
raw_sources.perspective: The best work happens under constraints; six-week cycles, small teams, and an appetite-driven approach to scope produce better outcomes than unlimited budgets, large teams, and feature-driven roadmaps.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder and CEO of 37signals (makers of Basecamp, HEY, and ONCE). Co-author of 'Rework', 'Remote: Office Not Required', and 'It Doesn't Have to Be Crazy at Work'. Pioneer of remote work and bootstrapped SaaS. Co-creator of the Shape Up methodology. Over 20 years building profitable software without venture capital.

raw_sources.name: Jason Lemkin
raw_sources.perspective: SaaS growth follows predictable stage-gate milestones from first 10 unaffiliated customers to T2D3 scaling; every revenue milestone triggers specific organizational hires and operational changes, and the founder who understands these thresholds makes better decisions at every stage.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder and CEO of SaaStr, the world's largest SaaS community (50,000+ attendees at annual conference). Two-time founder with successful exits (EchoSign acquired by Adobe for $400M+). Former VC at Storm Ventures. His SaaStr blog and podcast have published 5,000+ pieces of SaaS operational advice.

raw_sources.name: Jay Acunzo
raw_sources.perspective: Best practices are averages, and averages describe no one; the path to exceptional content is developing the intuition to ask 'what works best for us?' rather than copying what worked for someone else, because resonance, the urge to act that people feel when a message aligns with them, is what precedes conversion, not reach.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of Break the Wheel; creator and host of Unthinkable podcast (named must-listen by LinkedIn editors); former marketing leader at Google, HubSpot, and NextView Ventures (VP Brand); executive producer and storytelling consultant helping 200+ individuals and organizations differentiate their content.

raw_sources.name: Jill Konrath
raw_sources.perspective: The modern buyer is overwhelmed and frazzled, defaulting to inaction under cognitive overload, and the seller's primary job is to reduce the buyer's cognitive burden by being simple, invaluable, aligned, and priority-raising in every interaction.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'SNAP Selling,' 'Selling to Big Companies,' 'Agile Selling,' and 'More Sales Less Time,' keynote speaker on buyer psychology in B2B sales, former IBM and Xerox sales leader, named #1 Sales Expert to Follow by LinkedIn

raw_sources.name: Jim Hamilton
raw_sources.perspective: People do not buy because they understand how something works. They buy because they believe something new about their situation. The story's job is to shift a belief. The product's job is to act on the new belief.
raw_sources.credentials: Creator of the Email Storyselling framework; author of 'The Email Storyselling Playbook'. Known for teaching coaches, consultants, and online experts to convert email subscribers through narrative-driven selling. His method enables persuasive copy in approximately 15 minutes per email.

raw_sources.name: Jimmy Daly
raw_sources.perspective: A content strategy should be revealed by your business model, not invented from scratch; blogs are libraries, not publications, and the teams that treat them accordingly will compound returns while others burn out chasing a publishing calendar.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder and former CEO of Superpath, the largest community for content marketers; former VP of Growth at Animalz; writer at jimmydaly.com; advisor and contributor to Superpath blog and podcast.

raw_sources.name: Joe Pulizzi
raw_sources.perspective: The one thing killing content marketing is the absence of a content tilt; 99% of marketers create undifferentiated content, and without finding an area of little to no competition, breaking through is nearly impossible regardless of execution quality.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Content Marketing Institute (sold for ~$30M in 2015); author of Content Inc. and Epic Content Marketing (7 books, 500,000+ copies); founder of The Tilt and Content Entrepreneur Expo; coined the term 'content marketing' in 2001.

raw_sources.name: Joel Klettke
raw_sources.perspective: Stories are 22x more memorable than facts alone. But the best conversion copy combines story with data. Customer success stories are your most powerful sales asset because they let the prospect see themselves in someone else's transformation.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Case Study Buddy (seven-figure business); conversion copywriter who has written 150+ customer stories for B2B SaaS companies. Built an entire business model around the insight that customer narratives are the highest-converting content type in B2B.

raw_sources.name: John Barrows
raw_sources.perspective: Sales excellence lives in the execution layer, where the quality of every email, call, and meeting is determined by a systematic approach to research, personalization, and value delivery that can be taught, practiced, and measured.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of JB Sales, has trained sales teams at Salesforce, LinkedIn, Dropbox, Slack, and hundreds of high-growth SaaS companies, creator of the 'Filling the Funnel' and 'Driving to Close' training programs

raw_sources.name: Jonathan Stark
raw_sources.perspective: Hourly billing is a relic of factory-floor cost accounting that misaligns incentives, punishes expertise, and caps income; the antidote is value pricing anchored in the Why Conversation.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'Hourly Billing Is Nuts'. Former software consultant turned pricing advisor. Has coached 100+ solo consultants and small firms on value pricing. Publishes a daily email on pricing for independent professionals. Creator of the Value Pricing Bootcamp and Ditcherville community.

raw_sources.name: Josh Braun
raw_sources.perspective: Sales isn't about persuading people. It's about guiding prospects to persuade themselves. The moment you try to convince someone, you activate their resistance. Equal positioning (peer-to-peer) beats expert positioning (expert-to-prospect) every time.
raw_sources.credentials: Former Head of Sales at Basecamp (37signals); LinkedIn's most followed voice on B2B sales messaging; author of 'Selling Without Convincing'; creator of the Badass B2B Growth Guide. Known for inverting traditional sales copy methodology.

raw_sources.name: Julian Shapiro
raw_sources.perspective: Growth is not something you delegate as an afterthought; it must be designed into the product itself, and when it comes to paid acquisition, you must harden your funnel's conversion and referral rates before investing heavily into ads.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder of Demand Curve (largest professional educator in startup marketing) and Bell Curve (growth agency); former VP of Marketing at Webflow; growth marketing columnist at TechCrunch; created Velocity.js, the world's most popular web animation engine; angel investor through julian.capital.

raw_sources.name: Karl Sakas
raw_sources.perspective: Agency chaos is not inevitable; it is the predictable result of the owner doing everything themselves, and the cure is a deliberate operating system built on delegation, role clarity, and knowing when to stop growing.
raw_sources.credentials: Agency advisor at Sakas & Company. Has personally advised 600+ agencies across 36 countries on operations, growth, leadership, and exit planning. Author of 'Calm the Chaos: 10 Ways to Run a Better Agency' (4th book). Speaker on agency leadership. Background in agency operations.

raw_sources.name: Keenan (Jim Keenan)
raw_sources.perspective: All selling is about change, and the seller's only job is to discover and quantify the gap between the buyer's current state and desired future state; without a gap there is no sale, and without a large gap there is no large sale.

raw_sources.name: Leah Tharin
raw_sources.perspective: AI is collapsing the efficiency advantages that scaled SaaS companies built over a decade, forcing every scaleup back into startup mode where cross-functional growth teams, not siloed departments, must re-prove product-market fit from retention up.
raw_sources.credentials: Product-led growth strategist and advisor, former VP Product at Jua (AI weather foundation model) and Smallpdf, creator of the Leah's ProducTea newsletter, Maven PLG course instructor featured in university MBA programs

raw_sources.name: Lenny Rachitsky
raw_sources.perspective: The best product managers in an AI world become orchestrators who define what good looks like, delegate execution to AI agents, and maintain velocity through rapid prototyping rather than specification bloat.

raw_sources.name: Madhavan Ramanujam

raw_sources.name: Marcel Petitpas
raw_sources.perspective: Most agencies suffer from indigestion, not starvation; the path to profitability lies in measuring delivery margin and the three levers that drive it.

raw_sources.name: Marcus Andrews
raw_sources.perspective: The best product marketing creates a narrative so compelling that it becomes the lens through which customers see their own problems; narrative design is a repeatable, structured process borrowed from game design, not a creative lightning strike.
raw_sources.credentials: Former Product Marketing Group Lead and Narrative Designer at HubSpot (helped develop the Flywheel narrative), former Sr. Director of Product Marketing at Pendo, former product marketer at Google (AdWords and YouTube), co-creator and former host of the Product Marketing Experts podcast, author of 'Narrative Design for Business'

raw_sources.name: Marie Haynes

raw_sources.name: Mark Roberge

raw_sources.name: Matt Dixon

raw_sources.name: Matt Shumer

raw_sources.name: Momoko Price

raw_sources.name: Morgan Housel

Two-time winner of the Best in Business award from the Society of American Business Editors and Writers.

raw_sources.name: Nassim Nicholas Taleb
raw_sources.perspective: The central problem of modernity is that we have built fragile systems optimized for efficiency and prediction while living in a world dominated by randomness and fat-tailed distributions; the solution is not better prediction but building systems that gain from disorder.
raw_sources.credentials: Distinguished Professor of Risk Engineering at NYU Tandon School of Engineering. Former options trader and hedge fund manager. Author of the Incerto series: Fooled by Randomness, The Black Swan, Antifragile, Skin in the Game, and The Bed of Procrustes. His work on fat-tailed distributions and fragility has influenced fields from finance to public health to military strategy.

raw_sources.name: Nate
raw_sources.perspective: Skills are super-leveraged prompts requiring engineering rigor — treat prompts as code
raw_sources.credentials: Substack author, Claude Code educator, observed 100+ early Skills adopters

raw_sources.name: Naval Ravikant
raw_sources.perspective: Wealth creation is the product of specific knowledge, leverage, and judgment compounding over time; the new leverage (code and media) is permissionless and makes individual output asymmetric.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder and former CEO of AngelList. Prolific angel investor (Twitter, Uber, Postmates, 200+ startups). Author of the 'How to Get Rich (Without Getting Lucky)' tweetstorm. Subject of The Almanack of Naval Ravikant.

raw_sources.name: Neil Rackham
raw_sources.perspective: Sales effectiveness is an empirical science, not an art, and the specific verbal behaviors used during sales calls, particularly the types of questions asked, are measurable, learnable, and the primary determinant of outcomes in complex selling.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'SPIN Selling' (1988), 'Major Account Sales Strategy,' and 'Rethinking the Sales Force,' founder of Huthwaite International, conducted the largest empirical study of sales effectiveness ever (35,000+ calls, 12 years, 27 countries, $1M+ investment)

raw_sources.name: Nick Abraham
raw_sources.perspective: Cold email at scale is not about volume or copywriting but about layering intent signals, colleague references, and AI-generated personalization so that every automated email feels like it was manually researched and written for one person.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of LeadBird.io (150+ employees, 200+ active clients, 1.5M+ cold emails sent monthly), co-founder of Scrubby.io (email validation) and Quicklines.ai (AI personalization), recognized by Smartlead as the #2 most-replied-to account globally

raw_sources.name: Nik Sharma
raw_sources.perspective: Do less, better; early-stage DTC brands fail because they try to be world class at SEO, influencer marketing, paid ads, retail, and partnerships all at once, when they should master one funnel on one channel before expanding.
raw_sources.credentials: CEO of Sharma Brands (growth equity firm for DTC brands); former Head of DTC at Hint (grew ad spend from zero to $1M+/month); nicknamed 'The DTC Guy'; has built or advised category leaders including Feastables, Jolie, Brightland, Caraway, JuneShine, and Spritz Society; coined the 'performance branding' concept and the TRACE growth framework.

raw_sources.name: Packy McCormick
raw_sources.perspective: Everything is becoming technology, and the winners are those who 'run up the stack' -- leveraging newly commoditized capabilities as inputs to create higher-order human value rather than competing with machines at tasks that are being automated.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Not Boring (top tech/business newsletter, 250K+ subscribers), investor in Ramp/Scale AI/Replit/Varda, strategic advisor at Not Boring Capital

raw_sources.name: Park Howell
raw_sources.perspective: Story is not a nice-to-have marketing tactic but the operating system of human cognition. The ABT (And, But, Therefore) is the DNA of all compelling communication, and the 10-step Story Cycle System maps Joseph Campbell's Hero's Journey to business to place the customer as hero and the brand as mentor.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Business of Story. 40+ year veteran of advertising. EMMY Award winner. Author of 'Brand Bewitchery' and co-author (with Dr. Randy Olson) of 'The Narrative Gym for Business.' Has grown brands by as much as 600%. Clients include Dell, American Express, Hilton Hotels, Cummins, McCormick, and the United States Air Force.

raw_sources.name: Paul Jarvis
raw_sources.perspective: A company of one is not a freelancer with a website; it is a business that questions the assumption that growth is always good and deliberately optimizes for 'enough' rather than 'more'.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'Company of One: Why Staying Small Is the Next Big Thing for Business'. Designer and developer who ran his own company of one for 20+ years. Co-created Fathom Analytics, a privacy-first web analytics tool. Former web designer for clients including Microsoft, Mercedes-Benz, and Yahoo. Writer and podcaster on intentional business design.

raw_sources.name: Peep Laja
raw_sources.perspective: Sameness is the default in B2B; you cannot win on features alone because competitors will copy them, so the companies that win compete on messaging clarity, differentiation, and brand, not on product capabilities.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder and CEO of Wynter (B2B message testing platform) and CXL (the world's largest conversion optimization training institute); host of the 'How to Win' podcast; author of hundreds of data-backed articles on conversion optimization and B2B messaging; ran one of the most widely-read CRO blogs for a decade.

raw_sources.name: Peter Thiel
raw_sources.perspective: The most valuable companies and ideas are built by doing something entirely new, going from zero to one, rather than copying and incrementing what already exists; competition is for losers, and the goal is to build a monopoly on a truth that most people do not yet see.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder of PayPal and Palantir Technologies. First outside investor in Facebook. Partner at Founders Fund. Author of Zero to One: Notes on Startups, or How to Build the Future. Stanford Law graduate. One of the most influential technology investors and contrarian thinkers in Silicon Valley.

raw_sources.name: Pia Silva
raw_sources.perspective: The traditional agency model of multi-month projects with endless revisions is structurally broken; the fix is compressing delivery into paid-in-full intensives that protect your time, your margins, and your creative energy.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder of Worstofall Design, a branding agency that builds complete brands in 2-day intensives at $15K-$30K per project. Author of 'Badass Your Brand'. Creator of No BS Agency Mastery, an education program for small agency owners. Forbes contributor. Has taught thousands of agency owners the intensives model.

raw_sources.name: Pieter Levels
raw_sources.perspective: Ship fast, charge from day one, use boring technology, and never hire -- a single person with AI tools can build and operate multiple profitable products simultaneously by treating every idea as a cheap experiment rather than a precious venture.
raw_sources.credentials: Solo founder of Photo AI ($132K+ MRR), Nomad List, Remote OK, and 70+ other launched products, author of 'MAKE: The Indie Maker Blueprint' (10K+ copies sold), prominent digital nomad and indie hacker

raw_sources.name: Ramli John
raw_sources.perspective: Onboarding is the bridge between acquisition and monetization in product-led growth; if users do not reach the Eureka moment quickly, no pricing model can save you, and the onboarding-to-upgrade path must be designed as deliberately as the product itself.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Delight Path, former Managing Director of ProductLed, former Director of Content at Appcues. Author of 'Product-Led Onboarding' and 'EUREKA: The Product Onboarding Playbook for B2B Companies.' Has trained hundreds of PLG companies including Mixpanel, Zapier, Vidyard, and Bynder on user onboarding optimization. Host of the Growth Marketing Today podcast.

raw_sources.name: Rand Fishkin
raw_sources.perspective: Two-thirds of Google searches now end without a click, platforms algorithmically punish outbound links, and attribution infrastructure is breaking; the only rational response is zero-click marketing, which means creating standalone value where audiences already are and measuring lift rather than clicks.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder and CEO of SparkToro (audience research platform); founder of Moz (sold 2021); author of Lost and Founder and the upcoming Zero-Click Marketing; conducted the seminal zero-click search studies showing 58.5% of US Google searches end without a click.

raw_sources.name: Reforge
raw_sources.perspective: Growth is a system of interconnected loops and models, not a collection of tactics — sustainable growth requires understanding the quantitative mechanics of acquisition, retention, and monetization.
raw_sources.credentials: Premier growth strategy platform founded by Brian Balfour (ex-VP Growth at HubSpot) and Andrew Chen; programs have trained 10,000+ growth leaders; blog is the definitive resource on growth models, retention mechanics, and PLG strategy.

raw_sources.name: Robert Rose
raw_sources.perspective: Content strategy and content marketing are fundamentally different disciplines that most organizations conflate; content marketers draw on the wall with magic markers while content strategists use fine pens, and scaling content requires an operating model that manages content as a strategic enterprise asset, not a marketing tactic.
raw_sources.credentials: Chief Strategy Advisor at Content Marketing Institute since 2010; CEO of The Content Advisory (formerly Seventh Bear); co-author of Killing Marketing and Managing Content Marketing; has consulted with 500+ companies including 15 Fortune 100; fractional CMO and keynote speaker.

raw_sources.name: Ross Simmonds
raw_sources.perspective: The biggest waste in content marketing is not bad content but good content that nobody sees; most teams spend 80% of their time on creation and 20% on distribution when the ratio should be inverted, because distribution is the multiplier that determines whether content investment compounds or evaporates.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Foundation Marketing (B2B content marketing agency); author of Create Once, Distribute Forever; named one of the most influential marketers in the world by multiple publications; Harry Jerome Young Entrepreneur Award recipient; Top 50 CEOs in Atlantic Canada.

raw_sources.name: Rowan Tonkin
raw_sources.perspective: Marketing must speak the language of finance, not the language of marketing; the CMO who can translate pipeline metrics into financial outcomes earns strategic credibility, budget protection, and a seat at the revenue table.
raw_sources.credentials: Chief Marketing Officer at Planful, former sales and marketing leader across multiple global SaaS organizations, pioneer of the CMO-CFO alignment methodology, frequent speaker and author on marketing performance management and finance-marketing partnership

raw_sources.name: Sahil Bloom
raw_sources.perspective: The most powerful frameworks are simple enough to remember in the moment of decision; compounding applies to everything, not just money, and the greatest results come from the discipline of showing up when it is boring.
raw_sources.credentials: Creator of The Curiosity Chronicle newsletter (700K+ subscribers). Author of The 5 Types of Wealth. Former private equity investor at a leading growth equity fund. Stanford University athlete and graduate. One of the most-followed business thinkers on social media with frameworks reaching millions.

raw_sources.name: Sally Hogshead
raw_sources.perspective: Different is better than better. You don't learn how to be fascinating, you unlearn how to be boring. In a distracted, overcrowded world, brands win not by being the best but by being impossible to ignore. Fascination is the most powerful way to capture attention.
raw_sources.credentials: Creator of the Fascination Advantage system, the first personality and brand assessment that measures how others perceive you, based on research with over one million participants. Author of 'Fascinate: How to Make Your Brand Impossible to Resist' and 'How the World Sees You' (#1 Wall Street Journal, #2 New York Times bestseller). Named the #1 brand guru in the world by a major marketing survey. Hall of Fame speaker. Former advertising creative who won her first Clio Award at age 24. Clients include IBM, Twitter, Porsche, GE, Cisco, and New York University.

raw_sources.name: Sam Altman
raw_sources.perspective: The cost of intelligence is converging toward the cost of electricity, which means the winning strategy is not using AI itself but parlaying the advantage of new technology into a durable business with real value -- small teams with full ownership, niche focus, and relentless velocity.
raw_sources.credentials: CEO of OpenAI, former president of Y Combinator, investor in Stripe/Reddit/Airbnb/Instacart, author of 'How To Be Successful' and 'The Gentle Singularity'

raw_sources.name: Sam Kuehnle
raw_sources.perspective: Brand is the force multiplier that makes demand gen actually work; stop separating brand and demand in org charts, budgets, and strategy, and instead ask how to strengthen your brand coefficient.
raw_sources.credentials: VP of Marketing at Loxo; formerly at Refine Labs; publishes Sam's Marketing Meditations newsletter on Substack; known for data-driven demand gen and paid media strategy in B2B SaaS.

raw_sources.name: Sam Oh
raw_sources.perspective: Video SEO is a distinct discipline from traditional web SEO because the ranking algorithm optimizes for engagement signals (watch time, audience retention, click-through rate) rather than link authority, making content quality and audience understanding the primary levers rather than technical optimization.
raw_sources.credentials: VP of Marketing at Ahrefs, Director of Product Education at Ahrefs, built the Ahrefs YouTube channel into one of the most authoritative SEO education channels with 500K+ subscribers, author of Ahrefs' definitive guides on YouTube SEO and video SEO, previously grew multiple YouTube channels in different niches

raw_sources.name: Sangram Vajre
raw_sources.perspective: B2B marketing has been running on a B2C model for decades; the unit of value in B2B is the account, not the lead, and aligning every go-to-market function around account-level engagement, measurement, and expansion is not an alternative tactic but the structurally correct way to operate.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder of Terminus (ABM platform), author of 'ABM Is B2B,' 'Account-Based Marketing for Dummies,' and co-author of 'MOVE: The 4-Question Go-to-Market Framework,' creator of the FlipMyFunnel movement and conference

raw_sources.name: Sarah Levinger
raw_sources.perspective: Transactional data only tells half the story; the brands that win at paid advertising are the ones that uncover the emotional context behind customer decisions, using psychology-based creative to speak to identity, subconscious behavior, and personal narratives rather than demographics alone.
raw_sources.credentials: Forbes-featured consumer behavior analyst and creative strategist; founder of Tether Insights; has helped brands including Fabletics, Hexclad, True Classic, Obvi, Yitty, Kettle and Fire, and Slumberkins increase paid advertising ROI through psychology-based creative; 10+ years studying consumer psychology, economics, and behavioral science.

raw_sources.name: Scott Galloway
raw_sources.perspective: In the digital age, brand is more important than ever, not less. The most valuable companies in the world have mastered the art of appealing to our primal instincts. A luxury strategy is the most potent brand strategy: scarcity, irrational pricing, and an iconic founder create a margin structure that compounds over decades.
raw_sources.credentials: Professor of Marketing at NYU Stern School of Business. Serial entrepreneur: founded nine companies including L2 (brand analytics, sold to Gartner), Red Envelope, Prophet, and Section4 (now Section). Bestselling author of 'The Four,' 'The Algebra of Happiness,' 'Post Corona,' 'Adrift,' and 'The Algebra of Wealth.' Host of the Prof G Pod and Pivot podcasts. His weekly newsletter No Mercy / No Malice reaches millions. Board member of multiple public companies.

raw_sources.name: Seth Godin
raw_sources.perspective: Marketing is no longer about the stuff that you make, but about the stories you tell. The goal isn't to maximize your numbers. The goal is to be missed if you stopped. People don't buy goods and services. They buy relations, stories, and magic.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 20+ bestselling books including 'Purple Cow,' 'Permission Marketing,' 'Tribes,' 'This Is Marketing,' 'The Dip,' and 'Unleashing the Ideavirus.' Founded Yoyodyne (sold to Yahoo! for $30M in 1998). His daily blog at seths.blog is one of the most popular in the world. Inducted into the Direct Marketing Hall of Fame and the Marketing Hall of Fame. Founder of altMBA and The Akimbo Workshops.

raw_sources.name: Shane Parrish
raw_sources.perspective: The quality of your thinking determines the quality of your life; mental models from multiple disciplines give you the latticework to make better decisions and avoid stupidity.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Farnam Street (fs.blog). Author of The Great Mental Models book series (4 volumes). Host of The Knowledge Project podcast. Former Canadian intelligence analyst.

raw_sources.name: Shireesh Gupta
raw_sources.perspective: 35% of PMM time goes to stakeholder management — make it trackable by building a CRM-like system with Claude Code that scores relationships on frequency, depth, and reciprocity
raw_sources.credentials: Product Marketing Manager, remote team across multiple time zones

raw_sources.name: Simon Sinek
raw_sources.perspective: People don't buy what you do; they buy why you do it. And what you do simply proves what you believe. The goal is not to do business with everybody who needs what you have. The goal is to do business with people who believe what you believe. Leaders who start with why inspire action rather than manipulate it.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of five books including 'Start with Why,' 'Leaders Eat Last,' 'The Infinite Game,' 'Together is Better,' and 'Find Your Why.' His 2009 TED Talk 'How Great Leaders Inspire Action' is the third most-watched TED Talk of all time with 60+ million views. Adjunct staff member at the RAND Corporation. Founder of The Optimism Company (formerly Start With Why LLC). Teaches graduate-level strategic communications at Columbia University.

raw_sources.name: Steph Smith
raw_sources.perspective: Content is a compounding asset that rewards systematic thinking; the key to standing out is creating content that is either genuinely new or measurably better than everything else, AND distributing it better than the competition, and the explore-and-exploit method is how you discover which channels actually work.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of Doing Content Right and Doing SEO Right; formerly led growth at The Hustle (Trends product to millions in ARR); hosted the a16z podcast; worked at HubSpot; currently at Nvidia after leading growth at Groq; creator of Internet Pipes.

raw_sources.name: Steven Forth
raw_sources.perspective: A pricing model without a value model is adrift; true value-based pricing begins with Economic Value Estimation that quantifies the economic impact of your solution on the customer's P&L relative to their competitive alternative.
raw_sources.credentials: Co-founder and Managing Partner of Ibbaka, a strategic pricing advisory firm for SaaS companies. Named one of the top 10 pricing authorities in the world by OpenView Venture Partners. Co-author of numerous pricing frameworks building on Tom Nagle's foundational work. Decades of experience in pricing consulting across software and technology companies.

raw_sources.name: Swyx (Shawn Wang)
raw_sources.perspective: We are in the transition from Software 2.0 to Software 3.0, where AI engineers -- not traditional software engineers -- will build the majority of new applications, and the key discipline is agent engineering: designing systems where LLMs control the application's logic flow.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of Latent Space (top AI engineering newsletter/podcast, 10M+ readers), creator of AI Engineer Summit, former engineer at Vercel/Netlify/AWS, coined 'AI Engineer' as a distinct role

raw_sources.name: Tas Bober
raw_sources.perspective: Conversion is not the name of the game in B2B; consumption is. The goal of each section on a landing page is not to convert the user but to send them to the next section, and the brands that win are the ones that put their buyers first by being clear, transparent, and empathetic.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of The Scroll Lab and Delphinium Solutions; B2B digital marketing strategist specializing in paid ads landing pages for B2B SaaS; has helped companies like Calendly drive 235% revenue increases through digital presence optimization; managed 400+ websites across her career; 15 years in digital marketing.

raw_sources.name: Tiago Forte
raw_sources.perspective: Our brains are for having ideas, not storing them -- an external, organized knowledge system compounds over time and becomes the foundation for creative output, especially when AI can now search, synthesize, and surface that knowledge on demand.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of 'Building a Second Brain' and 'The PARA Method,' creator of the Building a Second Brain course (adopted by Genentech, Toyota, Inter-American Development Bank), productivity consultant

raw_sources.name: Tim Ferriss
raw_sources.perspective: What we fear doing most is usually what we most need to do; effectiveness is doing the things that get you closer to your goals, and efficiency is performing a given task in the most economical manner possible, and the two should never be confused.
raw_sources.credentials: Author of The 4-Hour Workweek, The 4-Hour Body, The 4-Hour Chef, Tools of Titans, and Tribe of Mentors (all New York Times bestsellers). Host of The Tim Ferriss Show podcast (900M+ downloads). Angel investor and advisor (Uber, Shopify, Alibaba, Facebook, Twitter). Named by Fast Company as one of the Most Innovative Business People.

raw_sources.name: Tim Soulo
raw_sources.perspective: Ranking number one is not about being smarter than competitors through tricks but about being worthy of ranking number one through genuine expertise, credible ideas, and product-focused content that lets users experience your product in their imagination.
raw_sources.credentials: CMO and Product Advisor at Ahrefs, 15+ years in digital marketing and SEO, architect of Ahrefs' content strategy that grew the blog to 1.5M+ monthly visitors, author of multiple data-driven SEO studies

raw_sources.name: TK Kader
raw_sources.perspective: An ICP is not a buyer persona — it is an account-level definition that must be operationalized as a living mandate in your CRM, sales meetings, and product roadmap, not a static slide deck.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of TK Kader's AI/SaaS GTM Program; SaaS growth strategist who has helped companies scale from $10M to $40M+ ARR; runs idealcustomerprofile.com as the definitive ICP resource.

raw_sources.name: Tommy Walker
raw_sources.perspective: Content operations at scale requires three axioms: a solid premise gets you 80% of the way there, decisions are made in Slack (meaning you must understand where organizational buying happens), and content is a form of social currency that people use to express their identity and enhance their professional standing.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder of The Content Studio, a content marketing consultancy for Fortune 1,000 companies and high-growth B2B startups; former Global Editor-in-Chief at QuickBooks (Intuit); first marketing hire at Shopify Plus; clients include LinkedIn, GoDaddy, and other enterprise brands; creator of Content Theory course on Maven.

raw_sources.name: Trish Bertuzzi
raw_sources.perspective: The sales development function is a precision operating system with six interdependent elements; optimizing any one without the others produces local improvements but systemic mediocrity.
raw_sources.credentials: Founder and CEO of The Bridge Group (20+ years of SDR consulting), author of 'The Sales Development Playbook,' publisher of the most widely referenced SDR metrics benchmarks in B2B SaaS

raw_sources.name: Vivian Jordan
raw_sources.perspective: AI clears the runway for competitive strategy — 8-10 hour teardowns compressed to under an hour using Claude Chrome for multi-channel analysis
raw_sources.credentials: AI Product Marketing and GTM Strategist, helps companies translate enterprise AI complexity into clarity

daily.title: What landed today, 2026-05-27
daily.summary: Five new insight cards, two new operators, one new synthesis pattern. Three independent specialists named structural failures in their respective lanes within 48 hours of each other.

daily.title: What landed today, 2026-05-25
daily.summary: Four new insights, zero new operators, three pattern updates. The AEO citation universe split into two distinct populations. Verification remained the rate limiter across every domain AI accelerated.

daily.title: Five insights, two themes. AI search rewired at the platform level. VOC craft is the answer.
daily.summary: Five new cards across five operators. Two themes: AI search architecture changed at the platform level (Mike King, Rand Fishkin, Barry Schwartz), and content that resonates is built from verbatim buyer language not synthesis (Maja Voje, Nicolas Cole).

daily.title: One level down: two operators on why surface-level matching misses what matters
daily.summary: Two new insights, two synthesis patterns updated, one new operator. One meta-theme ran through both: surface-level matching fails where depth is needed. Shreyas Doshi names it for strategy. Anthropic names it for security.

daily.title: AEO splits into three jobs, the judgment ceiling holds, and listening compounds
daily.summary: Nine new cards. AEO fractures into citation, traffic, and IP protection as three separate jobs. The judgment ceiling holds as execution costs drop. One strong data set on customer marketing as systematic listening.

daily.title: Five new insights: recovery floors, AI consensus, and task-driven minimalism
daily.summary: Five new cards from five operators. Rewind names the chilling effect that recovery gaps create on AI adoption. Packy McCormick and Nicolas Cole converge on distinctiveness as the post-AI scarcity from independent angles. Pawel Huryn and Chris Orlob arrive at the same minimalist discipline applied to skill acquisition and outreach sequencing.

daily.title: Earn the citation, hold the floor, retire the position metric
daily.summary: Four new cards from Lily Ray, Aleyda Solis, Eli Schwartz, and Aakash Gupta. Ray and Solis converge on the structural architecture of AEO visibility from opposite directions. Schwartz names the measurement construct that is dissolving beneath the whole category. Gupta gives a one-sentence test that changes how you scope a skills library.

daily.title: The reliability trap, stripping what does not compound, and context isolation
daily.summary: Seven new cards, one new pattern, three pattern extensions. Two themes surfaced independently across the week: reliability quietly degrading the oversight it depends on, and AI abundance shifting the scarce resource from output to judgment.

daily.title: AI implementation realism and AEO benchmarks, 2026-05-11
daily.summary: Four new cards across two themes: human checkpoints as the AI implementation separator (Pierri + Breunig convergence extending pat_verification-as-human-job), and AI discovery producing measurable signup volume (Poyar benchmark + Schwartz on Google's formalized citation surfaces). Two new operators: Drew Breunig, Barry Schwartz.

daily.title: Context engineering quantified, AEO continuity, volume versus curation
daily.summary: Four new cards from the week of May 10, 2026. Poyar and Voje survey 200 GTM operators and produce the first large-sample quantitative evidence for the context-not-capability thesis. Lily Ray frames AEO as an extension of SEO discipline, not a replacement. Swyx names curation as the quality lever in the AI-volume era. Anthropic Routines establishes the boundary between cloud-hosted and local agent execution.

daily.title: AEO becomes a page-experience problem; agentic architecture settles into named patterns
daily.summary: Eleven new cards, one new synthesis pattern, two pattern updates, four new operators. Four AEO practitioners converged on a unified experience-and-structure gate frame. Three themes from independent directions.

daily.title: Verification convergence: four operators, one platform answer
daily.summary: Nine new cards plus one extended pattern. The week's dominant signal: closing the verification loop is the production bottleneck in AI-native work. It converged across four independent operators, and Anthropic shipped the platform version of the same argument.

daily.title: Verification, judgement, scarcity, voice, three converging frames in one week
daily.summary: 15 new cards, 2 synthesis patterns, 4 new operators. Today's batch plus a back-audit of past digests (April 26 to May 4) for atomic claims that hadn't yet been ingested.
sources: Backfill audit, past digests April 26 to May 4

daily.title: Research scan v1-v6, depth-pilot to new-operator workflow
daily.summary: Six research-scan batches ran across the day, ingesting **134** insight cards, **13** synthesis patterns, **4** methodology playbooks, **2** contradictions, and **2** new operator profiles. The batches followed a depth-first protocol, fewer operators per pass, more substantive cards per operator, and capped with the new-operator workflow that produced the David Ogilvy and Dave Trott profiles.

Two specific re-attribution fixes for Sam Mallikarjunan and Sourav Mohanty. Only 1 new card landed; the day was about depth, not adds.

Three phases of data triage closed gaps the new UI exposed, fixed YAML parser, deduped 3 confirmed duplicates, patched 19 dead URLs and 16 operator externals, restored LinkedIn reactions, and ingested 182 operator profiles + 216 cards. Pass C orphan-ingestion ran in 6 batches; Curiosity backfill ran in 8 batches adding external links across hundreds of profiles; the audit pass closed 8 stubs, validated 30 URLs, repaired 73 cards.
